title: Sambar

overview.description: A South Indian lentil and vegetable stew made with toor dal, tamarind and sambar masala. Rich, tangy and deeply comforting.

method: Pressure cook the dal with the water and turmeric for 15 minutes, then allow the pressure to release naturally.
Mash the cooked dal until smooth.
Heat the oil or ghee over a medium heat.
Add the mustard seeds and allow them to pop.
Add the cumin seeds, curry leaves and asafoetida.
Add the onion and cook for 6–8 minutes until softened.
Stir in the tomatoes and cook until beginning to break down.
Add the butternut squash, carrots and green beans.
Stir in the sambar masala and cook for 30 seconds.
Simmer gently for 15 minutes until the vegetables are tender.

learning.did-you-know: Every South Indian household has its own preferred version of sambar, with different vegetables and spice blends depending on the region and season.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to pressure cook toor dal, prepare a South Indian tempering and balance tamarind with lentils and vegetables.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A nutritious one-pot meal that makes excellent use of pantry staples, frozen vegetables and the pressure cooker.
chapman-tips: Pressure cooking the dal gives the smoothest texture and saves a considerable amount of time.
Use whichever vegetables are in season or already in the freezer.
Sambar masala is worth buying rather than making for everyday cooking.
Add the tamarind towards the end of cooking to preserve its bright flavour.
notes.batch-cooking: Freeze in family-sized portions for up to 6 months.
notes.leftovers: Often tastes even better the following day.
